verdicts.detail: The documentation describes an MCP skill whose tools make external API calls to 12306/xiaobenyang.com. The outputs depend entirely on live API responses and cannot be determined from the documentation alone. The docs pin the parameter names and types but do not pin any specific output values, formats, or even the exact stdout produced by running these Python scripts directly (they are designed to be called as modules by an LLM agent, not as standalone CLI tools). There is no documented CLI invocation pattern or pinned output that would allow constructing verifiable test cases.

All CLI commands either depend on live API data (making return values unpredictable), or their exact output format/wording is not pinned by the documentation. The docs show a sample JSON structure but field values depend on real API responses. Error messages, status outputs for 'configure', and all data-returning commands produce values that cannot be determined from the docs alone.

The documentation describes agent-level behavior, interaction rules, and high-level CLI flags, but does not pin the exact stdout format, field names, or output structure of any individual tool file listed. The main entrypoint is scripts/main.ts (not one of the listed tool files), and the helper scripts (balance.js, status.js, normalize.js, validators.js, etc.) have no documented output format. Even for --dry-run or --json modes on main.ts, the exact JSON fields and structure are not specified in the documentation, making it impossible to construct a reliable golden test without guessing.

The documentation describes a complex report-generation system whose outputs depend entirely on live external data sources (akshare, yfinance, mx-data API, etc.) and the current date. The docs do not pin any specific stdout output for any invocation — they only describe file outputs (e.g., reports/morning/A股早报-YYYYMMDD.md) and internal architecture. There is no documented stdout format, no example output strings, and no deterministic behaviour that can be derived from the documentation alone without guessing. Even the test commands invoke pytest which produces non-pinnable output. Therefore no test cases can be constructed with unambiguous expected_stdout values.

The documentation pins only one specific output string (当前暂无可执行短线买入标的) for the no-recommendation case, but I cannot guarantee any particular date input will trigger that code path rather than a network/data error. All other outputs (scores, statistics, report formats) are described functionally but not with pinned formats, field names, or exact text. There is insufficient information to construct test cases with unambiguous expected stdout.

The documentation describes a multi-step conversational workflow and what the skill should do in response to API return values, but does not pin the exact stdout format of any individual script/tool. The action codes (1/2/3) for versionCheck.js are documented as inputs to skill logic, not as pinned stdout values. No tool's complete output format is specified well enough to construct a test case with unambiguous expected_stdout.

The documentation for scan_packages.py only states that its output format is described in 'the script header comment' (脚本头部说明), which I cannot see. The SKILL.md itself does not pin the exact stdout format, field names, ordering, or structure of the script's output. Without knowing the output format, I cannot construct any test case with an unambiguously predicted expected_stdout.
1 cold golden(s) diverged — UNCONFIRMED (expected bytes were inferred from docs, not author-pinned; likely a format guess). Review, do not accuse.

The documentation does not pin any specific output format, labels, field names, or response structure that would allow constructing unambiguous expected outputs without actually calling the API. Any test case would require guessing the output format or mocking the API, neither of which is supported by the documentation alone.

The documentation describes a skill whose daily_report.py script requires live API calls to an external service (REDFOX_API_KEY), network access, and real-time date logic. The script's exact stdout is not pinned by the documentation — the output format shown is for the AI agent's conversational response, not the raw stdout of daily_report.py itself. For testable parameters like --subscribe or --unsubscribe, the documentation does not specify what stdout these produce. There is no documented case where the exact bytes printed to stdout by daily_report.py are specified for any input that could be constructed without live network access and valid API credentials.

The documentation does not pin any exact stdout output for any CLI invocation. The cad_utils.py tool writes to an output file (not stdout). The batch_modify.py, batch_export.py tools' success/error messages are not documented at all. The generate_dwg.py is documented only as a Python module API, not a CLI with pinned stdout. There is no basis to construct test cases with unambiguous expected_stdout values.

The documentation specifies only the input payload format and the supported actions/arguments, but does not pin any output format, response structure, exact messages, field names, or values for any action. Without knowing the exact stdout produced by any invocation, it is impossible to construct test cases with unambiguously correct expected outputs.

The documentation specifies that handle() returns JSON with 'input_analysis' and 'disclaimer' fields, but does not pin the exact values of any fields, the full JSON structure, or any specific string content that would appear in the output. The usage scenarios describe conceptual expected outputs in natural language, not exact JSON values. Without knowing what values appear in the JSON fields, no test case can have an unambiguously predictable expected output.

All observable outputs depend on network state, wallet state, and API responses that cannot be predicted from the documentation alone.

The documentation does not pin the exact output format for any command. It shows usage examples but never specifies the exact stdout text, structure, labels, ordering, or formatting of responses to --add, --done, --list, --today, or --stats commands. Without knowing the precise output format (e.g., what text is printed when adding a task, what the list looks like, what stats output contains), any expected_stdout would be a guess and could cause false accusations.

There is no documented invocation signature, expected stdout text, or any other output that can be unambiguously derived from the documentation alone for any concrete test case.

The skill documentation describes a conversational AI tool that generates open-ended cultural intelligence guidance based on user prompts. The outputs are lengthy, qualitative, natural-language responses whose exact wording, structure, and formatting are not pinned by the documentation — only loose thematic descriptions of what the output should contain are given. There is no deterministic input→exact output mapping that could be verified byte-for-byte or even by a well-scoped regex or field check. Any test case I constructed would require guessing the exact phrasing, ordering, and formatting of the AI-generated response, which the docs explicitly do not pin.

The documentation describes the skill's behavior at a high level (generates Markdown/JSON reports in a reports/ directory, fetches from external data sources, etc.) but does not pin any specific stdout output for any invocation. The tool requires external network access and a YAML config file, and all report content depends on live fetched data. There is no documented deterministic stdout output for any command-line invocation that could be tested without guessing formatting, labels, or values.

The documentation describes a skill that fetches live data from an external API (60s AI news API). The docs only describe the invocation interface and encoding formats, not any specific response content.

The documentation does not pin the exact output of the tool — the output depends on live external API calls (wttr.in for weather and unspecified news sources for headlines), making it impossible to predict exact stdout values. The date, weather data, and news titles all vary at runtime, and no deterministic/offline mode is documented. Even the wrapper text (labels, emojis, ordering) could differ between runs. There is no single unambiguous, reproducible output that can be constructed from the documentation alone.

The scripts generate output files (e.g., /tmp/cost-report-YYYY-MM-DD.md) and depend on `openclaw sessions --all-agents --json` returning real session data. Without that data source, any generated report would be empty or error out. The documentation does not pin what the scripts print to stdout (as opposed to what they write to files), nor does it pin behaviour for edge cases like missing data or missing OPENAI_API_KEY in a way that would allow constructing a test with exact expected stdout. The example output shown in the docs is explicitly an example of file contents, not stdout, and is marked as illustrative rather than deterministic. There is no documented stdout output that can be predicted without the live OpenClaw environment.

The documentation describes the purpose and CLI arguments of report_manager.py and calc_accuracy.py but never pins the exact stdout format (JSON structure, key names, line ordering, labels, or whitespace) for any invocation. Fields like history_files, selected_output_file, and rolling accuracy values are referenced by name but their serialization format is not documented. Without knowing whether output is JSON, plain text, key=value, or another format, no test case can be constructed with an unambiguously correct expected_stdout.

The documentation does not pin the exact stdout output for any of the tool invocations. The usage examples show command-line invocations but do not specify what text is printed to stdout. The Python code examples show internal API usage and one print statement pattern (e.g., 'Average IoU: {report["iou"]:.2f}') but the actual values depend on input data and runtime computation, not fixed values. Without knowing the exact output format, labels, values, or even whether the scripts produce any stdout at all for given inputs, it is impossible to construct test cases with unambiguous expected outputs.

The documentation describes a long-running background script that sends Telegram notifications, performs brew cask operations, health checks, and rollbacks. It produces no stdout (it is designed to be run with >/dev/null 2>&1). There is no documented stdout output whatsoever to verify in a test harness. The script's behaviour depends entirely on external system state (brew, launchctl, openclaw binary, Telegram, /Applications, /opt/homebrew, etc.) and produces no pinned stdout values that could be checked.

The documentation does not pin any exact stdout output for any invocation. The documentation only shows example output for live price queries that depend on real-time web scraping results (prices from Taobao, JD, Amazon, etc.). The version field exists in the YAML front-matter but the docs do not pin how or whether it is printed to stdout. Abstaining is correct here.

Without knowing the exact output format (labels, prefixes, field names, whitespace), any expected_stdout would be a guess, risking false accusations. Even regex/last-token approaches would require knowing what tokens appear in the output, which is not specified.
